races.name: O Gran Camiño – Stage 2
races.description: Stage 2 of O Gran Camiño travels 148.6km from Vilalba — deep in the interior plains of Terra Chá, Lugo — northwest to the coastal town of Barreiros on the A Mariña coast, finishing right by the beach. The official race site calls it 'a fun stage'. Despite ~2,000m of elevation gain, it is the flattest stage of the week. The key difficulty comes in the final 25km: a series of climbs culminates in the Cat. 3 Alto do Noceda with around 10km to go, before the road descends to the seaside finish. The downhill finale means this suits puncheurs who can attack on the climbs, or aggressive riders who can hold a gap to the line — not a straightforward bunch sprint, but not a climbers' stage either. The GC gaps from Stage 1 are small (leader Johansen at 17:43; Yates 6th at +40s), meaning a serious GC attack here is unlikely, but climbers will be watching each other.

gc.1: Julius Johansen (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) – wears leader's jersey
gc.notes: Full GC not retrieved; Yates 6th at +0:40. Nordhagen 4th. Ivan Romeo punctured mid-ITT, lost significant time, out of GC contention.

key_casualties: Iván Romeo (Movistar) — punctured on the cobbled coastal section mid-ITT, lost substantial time, effectively out of GC
Davide Formolo — mechanical issue on same section

key_climbs.name: Alto do Noceda
key_climbs.notes: Final significant climb; descent to finish on the coast after summit

races.rider_notes: Julius Johansen (UAE, 26y, Danish) leads in yellow after a stunning ITT that surprised even himself — 'I was 30 watts under what I expected, but almost two minutes faster.' He will want to control rather than attack today. Adam Yates (UAE) is the GC leader's key teammate and overall target at +40s — close enough to target the GC win but needing the mountain stages to make the real move. Nordhagen (Visma) is the best-placed non-UAE GC rider and will look to be aggressive. The finish after a descent to the coast means pure climbers cannot simply ride away; a puncheur with finishing punch or a small group scenario is most likely. Ivan Romeo's GC hopes were wrecked by his Stage 1 puncture.

broadcasts.notes: TVG free regional broadcast. Available via streaming at crtvg.es.

data_confidence.notes: Stage 2 on April 15 confirmed: CyclingUpToDate Stage 1 result article (dated April 15) says 'O Gran Camiño heads into Stage 2'; cyclingstage.com says 'The next stage is the flattest of the week, and after a hilly stage on Thursday, the queen stage is scheduled for Friday' — confirming Stage 2 = Wednesday April 15, Stage 3 = Thursday April 16, Stage 4 = Friday April 17. Stage 2 route: Vilalba → Barreiros, 148.6km confirmed by Cyclingnews and UAE team page. Elevation ~2,000m per CyclingFlash preview. Alto do Noceda (Cat.3) with ~10km to finish confirmed by UAE team info ('Alto do Noceda awaits, but the finish is after a descent'). UK start time 11:30 BST confirmed by HBO Max schedule page showing Stage 2 on Apr 15 at 11:30am. US time of 7:30 AM EDT (Amazon Prime showed Stage 2 at 'Wed, Apr 15 7:30 AM'). Start time CEST estimated from broadcasts — ~12:00 CEST (~10:00 UTC). Stage 1 GC standings from CyclingUpToDate results article: Johansen 17:43, Reis +15, Oliveira +16, Nordhagen 4th, Yates 6th at +40s.

races.name: Ronde van Limburg
races.finish_type: Bunch sprint / uphill drag (final hundreds of metres slightly rising, two key bends in last 1.5km)
races.description: The 78th edition of Ronde van Limburg — a Flanders Classics 1.1 race and the traditional bridge between Paris-Roubaix and the Ardennes week. Starting at the Kolonel Dusartplein in Hasselt (where Merlier won the 2024 European Championship), the race traverses 178.4km through Limburg and Haspengouw with 9 classified climbs and 8 cobbled sections before finishing on the Elisabethwal in Tongeren-Borgloon, Belgium's oldest city. Route structure: an opening loop through Bilzen-Hoeselt (Glinberg 1km @ 4.7%; Keiberg 600m @ 4.7% twice; Maastrichterallee cobbles 700m @ 4.2% twice), then three laps of the 38.2km 'Limburg loop' used at the 2024 European Championships — each lap featuring Kolmontberg (1.5km @ 3.1%), Zammelenberg (800m @ 4.3%), Manshoven cobbles (1km @ 2.4%) and Op de Kriezel cobbles (1.7km @ 3%). The final 12km after the last cobbles is mostly flat with crosswind exposure, then two crucial bends in the last 1.5km before the mildly uphill finishing straight on Elisabethwal. Suits punchy sprinters with a classics engine rather than pure bunch sprinters. Reigning champion Milan Fretin (2025 winner) is absent — he fractured his elbow at Scheldeprijs. Previously on the calendar on Wednesdays between Roubaix and Brabantse Pijl; Brabantse Pijl has now moved to Friday, cementing Ronde van Limburg in this slot.
races.rider_notes: Tim Merlier (Soudal Quick-Step) is the dominant favourite. Won Scheldeprijs last week for his 3rd consecutive title in only his 2nd race back from a knee injury. Also won Ronde van Limburg in 2021. Much of the course follows the roads where he won the 2024 European Championship. Soudal DS Kevin Hulsmans: 'The course has difficult sections — short climbs, cobbles, and wind in the final laps could play a decisive role.' Dylan Groenewegen (Unibet Rose Rockets) has withdrawn — still feeling effects of his Scheldeprijs crash. Groenewegen was the 2024 winner and had been planned as Merlier's main rival. Milan Fretin (2025 winner, Cofidis) is absent with an elbow fracture from the Scheldeprijs. Without Groenewegen and Fretin, Merlier's path to victory is significantly clearer. Jasper Stuyven (Soudal) offers a more attacking option if the pure sprint doesn't materialise. 6 WorldTeams present: Lidl-Trek, Jayco AlUla, Lotto-Intermarché, Bahrain Victorious, Soudal Quick-Step, Alpecin-Premier Tech.

broadcasts.notes: Free-to-air on VRT1 / Sporza from 15:15 CEST (13:15 UTC).
Live on TNT Sports and HBO Max. Exact start time not confirmed — estimated from Eurosport/Sporza pattern (15:30 CEST = 13:30 UTC). Plans from £25.99/month.

Merlier also won this race in 2021 and won the 2024 European Championship on overlapping roads.

recent_results.race: O Gran Camiño Stage 1 ITT (2.1) – A Coruña, 14.8km
recent_results.notes: Johansen, 26, Danish, delivered the biggest win of his pro career — 'I was 30 watts under what I expected, but almost two minutes faster.' UAE's Yates sits in 6th overall at +40s — close enough that the mountains can still deliver the overall. Ivan Romeo (Movistar) punctured on the cobbled coastal section and lost major time. Formolo also had mechanical issues. Nordhagen (Visma) set an early benchmark but was ultimately 4th.

Fastest ever edition (48.91kph). Van Aert won a two-up sprint after Pogačar's legs gave out. Van der Poel suffered multiple mechanicals in Arenberg — almost bridged back but finished 4th. Van Aert dedicated win to Michael Goolaerts. First Belgian win since 2019; Visma's first since 1984.
Koch won from a three-up sprint — biggest win of her career. Kopecky 4th after puncture/crash cost her podium shot.

notes: Julius Johansen's O Gran Camiño ITT win is his biggest career result to date. He set personal-best power figures for a time that he expected to be slower — ideal form for UAE's Giro d'Italia build-up.

Adam Yates (+40s after Stage 1) is the GC target for UAE and is well placed heading into the mountain stages. The team have a tactical luxury: Johansen is a legitimate jersey contender AND a Yates support option.
Iván Romeo's puncture was the key story of Stage 1 — he was setting the fastest intermediate time when the mechanical struck on the cobbled coastal road section. A damaging loss of GC time.
The calendar is in a quiet stretch between the spring classics (now over) and the Ardennes week starting Friday April 17 with Brabantse Pijl. O Gran Camiño is the only WT/Pro-level action until then.

Brabantse Pijl (April 17) marks the real opening of the Ardennes sequence: men's and women's 1.Pro races in Belgium, then Amstel Gold Race (April 19, WT), La Flèche Wallonne (April 22, WT), and Liège-Bastogne-Liège (April 26, WT Monument).

upcoming.notes: Carballo → Padrón, 169km, 2,470m climbing. Rolling Coruña region. Pico Muralla (Cat. 2, 10.7km @ 3.8%, steep start at 6.1% for first 3km) summits 30km before finish. Flat to sprint finish after descent — suits attackers or strong sprinters. Key day for breakaways.

upcoming.race: O Gran Camiño Stage 4 – Queen Stage (2.1)
upcoming.notes: Xinzo de Limia → Alto Cabeza de Meda, 145.7km, ~3,000m climbing crammed into last 55km. Cabeza de Meda climbed twice from different sides; Alto de Rodicio (5km @ 6.6%) between. Final climb 5.1km @ 9.4%. This decides the GC.
162.5km, Beersel (Huizingen) → Overijse. 21 climbs. Three local circuits with Hertstraat, Moskesstraat, Holstheide and S-Bocht Overijse. Reigning champion: Remco Evenepoel (2025).
125.7km, Lennik → Overijse. 19 climbs. Start 11:25 CEST. Reigning champion: Elisa Longo Borghini (2x winner).
First Ardennes Monument. Classic puncheur finish.
La Doyenne — final Monument of spring.

issues: Stage 2 date: Cyclingnews stages page lists Stage 2 (Vilalba–Barreiros) with date 2026-04-14, but this is the same systematic -1 day error as Stage 1 (which ran April 14 despite being listed as April 13). All evidence — HBO Max UK (Stage 2 at 11:30am April 15), Amazon Prime (Stage 2 at 7:30 AM EDT April 15), cyclingstage.com ('hilly stage on Thursday' = Stage 3 = April 16, implying Stage 2 = April 15) — confirms Stage 2 is today, April 15. Using April 15.

US start time: Amazon Prime listing showed Stage 2 at 7:30 AM EDT. This is earlier than Stage 1 (9:30 AM EDT per FloBikes). Both may be broadcast start times rather than race start times. Using 7:30 AM EDT (11:30 UTC) as shown by Amazon Prime for US.
Stage 2 distance: Cyclingnews stages page shows 148.6km; HBO Max UK listing says 158.5km (likely includes neutralised sections). Using 148.6km as the official race distance.
Full Stage 1 GC standings not available — only top positions retrieved from CyclingUpToDate article. Yates confirmed 6th at +40s from the same source.
